summary: Jeff Atwood argues that developers should avoid both over-specialization and becoming unfocused generalists, instead striving to become 'generalizing specialists' who master a few areas while maintaining broad competence. He responds to criticism of his earlier post about cultivating non-technical skills, clarifying that the goal is becoming a better software developer, not abandoning technical expertise. The post highlights how teams with rigid specialists create bottlenecks where people wait on 'the database guy' or 'the testing guy' instead of delivering complete features. Atwood emphasizes that in agile environments, the measure of success is working features, not completed individual tasks, making cross-functional skills essential.
key_points: A generalizing specialist is 'jack-of-all-trades and master of a few' - distinctly different from a generalist who masters none
Over-specialization creates process smells in teams, with incomplete work piling up as people wait for specialists
Agile teams measure delivery by working features, not individual tasks, which specialists alone cannot deliver
Cultivating non-technical skills serves the goal of becoming a better software developer, not abandoning technical work
Growth requires regularly exceeding your comfort zone and learning new skills as team needs change
money_quotes: Cultivating non-technical skills is, first and foremost, about becoming a better software developer.
If you wanted to be rich, famous, and get girls, then you picked the wrong profession. I'm sorry I had to be the one to break this to you.
A generalist is a jack-of-all-trades but a master of none, whereas a generalizing specialist is a jack-of-all-trades and master of a few. Big difference.
A common process smell in new agile teams is more and more stories/backlog items incomplete at the end of the iteration.
Specialists typically don't deliver working features, thus the problem.
I see too many software developers burrowing themselves deeper and deeper into the same skillsets and specialties.
To be a better software developer, grow from a specialist to a generalizing specialist.
key_insight: The path to becoming a better software developer lies not in deeper specialization or broad superficiality, but in mastering a few areas while continuously stretching to learn complementary skills that help deliver complete, working features.
